entities.description: A keyboarded and structurally marked electronic text derived from the selected portion of Herrtage's 1879 edition. The encoding serializes printed parallel upper and lower page zones together at story-group level.
entities.label: University of Michigan Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse item GRom
notes: The XML says Seconr keyboarded the text, five percent was proofed to a stated one-error-per-20,000-character specification, Kenneth Church encoded it, and Catherine Paul reviewed the markup.
The language declaration says only Latin, but direct text inspection establishes Middle English as primary, with embedded Latin and modern English editorial matter.

bibliographic.citation: Sidney J. H. Herrtage, ed., The Early English Versions of the Gesta Romanorum, Early English Text Society, Extra Series 33 (London: N. Trübner & Co., 1879).
bibliographic.series: Early English Text Society, Extra Series 33
bibliographic.title: The Early English Versions of the Gesta Romanorum

entities.description: Herrtage's edited compilation of multiple Middle English manuscript streams, revised from Madden's 1838 edition, with eleven full Kk.1.6 parallel versions and eight additional stories associated with de Worde's print.
entities.label: Herrtage, Early English Versions of the Gesta Romanorum (1879)
notes: The encoded work portion is the title-page representation and printed pp. 1-444. The complete volume also contains contents, introduction, comparative table, notes and illustrations, appendix, glossary, and index.
Miss Lucy Toulmin Smith collated most proof sheets against Harley MS 7333 and Additional MS 9066, as Herrtage acknowledges.

rights.basis: The edition was first published in 1879, well before the current United States public-domain cutoff for published works described by the U.S. Copyright Office.
rights.component: Herrtage's 1879 transcription, arrangement, introduction, notes, glossary, index, and other editorial content
rights.jurisdiction: United States

entities.description: The principal manuscript source for Herrtage's Text I. The current British Library record places the Gesta Romanorum on ff. 150r-203r within a Leicester manuscript of the second half of the fifteenth century.
entities.label: British Library, Harley MS 7333
notes: Herrtage dated the manuscript about 1440; the current British Library catalog's broader 1450-1499 date is used for the physical witness.

entities.description: The manuscript source for parallel versions in Text I and the principal source for Text II. Herrtage describes an 87-leaf parchment manuscript written by two hands and presented to the British Museum by the Rev. William Conybeare in 1832.

holding.notes: Current holding is accepted from the established shelfmark and historical edition, but an exact current public catalog record was not recovered in the dated search.

entities.label: British Library, Additional MS 9066
notes: The XML's story-local milestones range through Herrtage's foliation to leaf 87 verso, but the relation scopes do not turn these printed milestones into a current catalog description of the whole codex.
One XML BIBL label says 'Harl. MS. 9066'; its surrounding Additional stream establishes that as an encoding or source-label error, not a separate Harley witness.
Herrtage reports two scribal hands, 96 stories in the volume, and a Gesta proper followed by related narrative material.
Physical description awaits confirmation from a current British Library item record or direct inspection.

entities.description: A fifteenth-century multi-text manuscript whose Gesta Romanorum section occupies ff. 216r-242v and contains thirty-two tales without moralizations. Herrtage prints eleven full Kk.1.6 parallels in Text II and consults the manuscript comparatively.

holding.notes: The Cambridge-hosted M. R. James handlist description is exact scholarly evidence for Kk.1.6, but it is not represented here as a current item-level holding catalog record.

entities.label: Cambridge University Library, MS Kk.1.6
notes: James describes the Gesta text as akin to Additional MS 9066 and notes Madden's use of it.
No public complete CUDL, IIIF, PDF, or other manuscript image delivery was verified during the dated search.
James reports 28-33 lines per page and brown calf binding.
Richard Fox writes or rubricates part of the manuscript and supplies the Gesta closing rubric.

entities.description: The early English print against which Herrtage says Madden's printed Text III readings were checked. The schema type scholarly_edition is used for this historical printed textual witness, not to imply modern critical editing.

holding.notes: ESTC explicitly records the St John's copy and shelfmark, but its external bibliographic record is not presented as a St John's item-level catalog URL. USTC also lists a Cambridge University Library holding without a shelfmark; that additional holding requires confirmation.

entities.label: Wynkyn de Worde, Gesta Romanorum (ESTC S4864; USTC 501090)
notes: Herrtage says the print resembles Harley MS 5369 in story selection, order, and peculiarities; this does not prove that the extant Harley manuscript was its direct exemplar.
The eight Text III stories are not a complete reproduction of the de Worde print.

physicalDescription.format: Early printed book with illustrations and an illustrated title page
notes: Herrtage describes the St John's copy as perfect but closely cropped.
The current ESTC and USTC copy lists differ from Herrtage's historical claim that the St John's copy was unique.

next_steps: Send the exact shelfmark and Herrtage provenance to British Library Reference Services and request the current item URI and full description.
Ask whether a complete internal digital, microfilm, or photographic surrogate exists and whether it can be supplied.
If imaging is possible, request the complete physical object, not only Herrtage's selected story loci, and obtain written item-specific reuse terms.
Replace Herrtage-only physical details with current curatorial evidence where they differ; retain both descriptions when historically meaningful.
openQuestions.question: What is the current British Library item record, physical description, foliation, consultation status, and complete-surrogate availability for Additional MS 9066?

next_steps: Ask the British Library whether the unavailable Harley digital object can be restored or supplied and request all physical surfaces if new photography is required.
Ask Cambridge University Library whether complete Kk.1.6 photography already exists and request the entire 247-leaf codex if supply is feasible.
For each deliverable, record exact source URL or order provenance, checksum, byte size, coverage, work portion, and written reuse terms before repository redistribution.
openQuestions.question: Can complete, reusable, item-specific facsimiles be obtained for Harley MS 7333 and Cambridge MS Kk.1.6, including full physical-object context rather than only the Gesta loci?

next_steps: Consult J. J. Gwara's 2013 dating argument and any subsequent bibliographical work before normalizing to one exact year.
Ask USTC and Cambridge University Library to identify the unshelfmarked CUL holding and determine whether it is a distinct surviving copy, a reproduction, or legacy catalog data.
Ask St John's whether complete photography exists and obtain explicit reuse terms; do not treat EEBO subscription access or the 1974 facsimile as open image licensing.
Compare the eight Text III passages directly with the original or complete facsimile to document Herrtage's corrections to Madden.
openQuestions.question: How should the de Worde print's revised 1507-1508? dating, USTC's [1510] convention, and the differing current copy lists be reconciled, and can a complete reusable image set of St John's A.2.18(4) be obtained?

next_steps: Retry the item in an ordinary interactive browser or ask U-M Digital Collections for the current GRom rights record.
Preserve the embedded restriction unless the provider supplies a clear item-specific interpretation or replacement statement.
Keep rights in Herrtage's public-domain edition, U-M's later transcription and markup, the checked-in XML, and third-party scan derivatives as separate layers.
openQuestions.question: What current item-specific rights statement does U-M apply to GRom, and does it supersede or clarify the redistribution restriction embedded in the XML?

next_steps: Audit the 1879 footnotes and typographic cues against the XML NOTE1 sequence and record the source signa used by Herrtage.
Compare the apparatus with Madden's 1838 edition and with complete images of Harley 7333, Additional 9066, Kk.1.6, and de Worde when accessible.
Do not assign the generic variant notes wholesale to Kk.1.6 or any other witness without note-level evidence.
openQuestions.question: Can each of the 1,347 XML NOTE1 elements marked TYPE=variant be attributed to a specific manuscript, Madden reading, printed correction, or other editorial source?

reviewNotes: The intellectual boundary is Herrtage's 1879 edited multi-version compilation, not the abstract Latin Gesta Romanorum, a homogeneous medieval English work, or one physical witness.
The 144 edition-level story groups comprise 70 Text I groups, 66 Text II groups, and eight Text III stories; parallel witness streams are not counted again as independent story groups.

Herrtage says the 1879 volume is far from a simple reprint of Madden: he twice collated the text with manuscripts, rearranged parallel versions, distinguished expansions, and revised readings and apparatus.

The XML has 1,347 NOTE1 elements marked TYPE=variant, but their immediate source is not encoded note by note. They are therefore documented as edition apparatus rather than assigned wholesale to Kk.1.6 or another witness.

Current ESTC dates de Worde's print to 1507-1508?, superseding the older c. 1510 convention retained by USTC, Herrtage, and the XML; both statements are preserved with their different evidential bases.

The current British Library catalog supplies an exact public record for Harley MS 7333 but targeted exact-shelfmark searches did not recover a current item record for Additional MS 9066. Herrtage remains the direct descriptive evidence for the latter pending curatorial confirmation.

The checked-in U-M-derived XML embeds an express redistribution restriction. Public-domain status of the 1879 edition does not silently settle rights or provider terms in the electronic encoding or scan files.

lineage.summary: The repository artifact is the pinned CME copy of University of Michigan item GRom, an edition-level electronic encoding of Sidney J. H. Herrtage's 1879 Early English Text Society compilation. Herrtage re-edited Frederic Madden's 1838 work against British Library Harley MS 7333 and Additional MS 9066, Cambridge University Library MS Kk.1.6, and the St John's College copy of Wynkyn de Worde's early-sixteenth-century Gesta Romanorum. The encoded selection retains a constructed title-page representation and printed pp. 1-444: Text I has 70 Harley-based story groups, including parallel Additional versions; Text II has 66 Additional-based groups, including eleven full parallel Kk.1.6 versions; Text III adds eight stories checked against de Worde. Most front matter and all later notes, appendix, glossary, and index are omitted. A complete 613-page Google/Internet Archive scan of the 1879 volume and its OCR, scandata, and metadata derivatives are cached; no complete public manuscript facsimile was verified for any of the three manuscript witnesses or for the St John's early print.

catalogSummary.author: Anonymous Middle English translators and adapters
catalogSummary.title: Early English Versions of the Gesta Romanorum

catalogingSubject.label: Early English Versions of the Gesta Romanorum
catalogingSubject.scope_note: Sidney J. H. Herrtage's 1879 edited compilation of multiple Middle English Gesta Romanorum versions: 70 Text I story groups based on Harley MS 7333 with selected Additional MS 9066 parallels, 66 Text II groups based on Additional MS 9066 with eleven full Kk.1.6 parallels, and eight Text III stories checked against Wynkyn de Worde's early print. The cataloged unit is not the abstract Latin Gesta tradition, a single medieval translation, any one physical witness, Madden's distinct 1838 edition, the complete 1879 scholarly volume beyond the edited-text compilation, the U-M encoding, the repository XML, or a facsimile.

contentParts.label: Text I: Harley MS 7333 with Additional MS 9066 parallel versions
notes: A group with a parallel Additional stream remains one edition-level story group, not two numbered tales.
The Additional streams are non-contiguous selections and are therefore not assigned one misleading continuous manuscript-folio range here.
The two mixed groups contain twenty-two ungrouped verse-line elements; brief embedded verse does not change the section's dominant prose character.
contentParts.summary: Seventy story groups whose principal streams follow Harley MS 7333, with selected second versions from Additional MS 9066 set alongside the corresponding Harley stories. Narratives are generally followed by Christian moralizations, and the edition preserves manuscript leaf and column milestones.

contentParts.label: Text II: Additional MS 9066 with Kk.1.6 parallels
notes: The eleven full Kk.1.6 streams occur in XML DIV1.333, DIV1.338, DIV1.343, DIV1.348, DIV1.353, DIV1.358, DIV1.366, DIV1.371, DIV1.376, DIV1.381, and DIV1.386, within printed pp. 327-359.
The section's two mixed groups contain sixteen and four verse lines respectively, for twenty Text II BODY L elements.
Parallel Kk.1.6 streams do not increase the 66-group edition inventory.
contentParts.summary: Sixty-six story groups principally based on Additional MS 9066. Eleven opening-region groups print full parallel versions from Cambridge MS Kk.1.6, whose complete Gesta section contains thirty-two tales without moralizations; the remaining edition apparatus records further comparison without making every generic variant note witness-specific.

contentParts.label: Text III: Eight stories from Wynkyn de Worde's edition

locators.value: selected passages represented from Aii through Liv

notes: Text III is a selected eight-story section, not a complete facsimile or diplomatic transcription of the 82-leaf de Worde book.

Herrtage's acknowledgment supports collation against the original print; it does not establish direct transcription of the complete print or prove that Harley MS 5369 was de Worde's physical exemplar.
contentParts.summary: Eight additional prose stories presented from Madden's earlier printed text after comparison with the St John's copy of Wynkyn de Worde's Gesta Romanorum. The XML structurally separates narrative and moralization divisions and sometimes supplies Moral headings where the source typography is unclear.

notes: This dates Herrtage's cataloged editorial compilation, not the medieval translations, manuscript copies, or de Worde print.
First publication of Herrtage's revised compilation as EETS Extra Series 33.

extent.description: The edition-level inventory counts 70 Text I story groups, 66 Text II groups, and eight Text III stories. Four groups contain forty-two ungrouped BODY L elements and are classified as mixed; the other 140 are prose-only. The XML has 137 BODY DIV1 elements because Texts I and II each use one DIV1 per story group while Text III uses one section-level DIV1 containing eight DIV2 stories. It also has 180 DIV2, 162 DIV3, 692 PB, 562 MILESTONE, 325 P, 42 BODY L, 1,386 NOTE1, and two NOTE elements. These XML counts are representation metrics, not additional intrinsic stories.

formStatements.description: A prose exemplum and moralized-tale compilation. Four of 144 story groups contain forty-two ungrouped verse-line elements in the body, but those brief embedded passages do not change the dominant whole-work form.

genres.label: Exemplum collection
notes: U-M's official browse page places the item under Exempla, and the stories characteristically pair narratives with moralizations.

genres.label: Tale collection
notes: The edition groups 144 narrative presentations in three source-defined sections rather than one authorial continuous narrative.

genres.label: Religious didactic prose
notes: Moralizations repeatedly interpret secular, classical, legendary, and marvelous narratives as Christian instruction.

languageStatements.label: Middle English
notes: The XML LANGUSAGE omits Middle English, but direct inspection and the edition title and contents establish it as primary.

scope.description: Narrative and moralization texts in all three edited sections

languageStatements.label: Latin
notes: Latin is genuinely embedded but is not the sole or primary language, despite the incomplete XML header declaration.

scope.description: Rubrics, narrative headings, biblical and learned quotations, formulas, and moralization phrases embedded in the Middle English texts

languageStatements.label: English
notes: Modern English editorial matter is not treated as a medieval co-primary language.

scope.description: Modern editorial title-page wording, supplied story descriptions and headings, apparatus, and encoding notes represented in the cataloged edition layer

notes: Work metadata catalogs Herrtage's 1879 edition-level compilation; physical holdings, access routes, facsimile completeness, rights, and digital-production layers remain in the linked lineage manifest.

The title-page representation is part of the encoded selection but is editorial apparatus rather than a fourth intrinsic content section; the content inventory therefore has three sections and 144 story groups.
The XML's Latin-only language declaration is incomplete. Direct inspection supports Middle English as primary, Latin as embedded, and modern English as editorial.
The 137 BODY DIV1 count is not a 137-story total: Text III uses one section DIV1 containing eight DIV2 stories, yielding 70 + 66 + 8 = 144 edition-level groups.
Parallel witness streams are structurally visible but are not double-counted as additional numbered stories.

The exact current catalog record and complete-facsimile status of Additional MS 9066 remain unresolved; historical Herrtage evidence is explicitly labeled rather than presented as confirmed-current catalog data.
The 1974 Exeter facsimile and EEBO reproduction are downstream consultation surrogates, not sources used by Herrtage in 1879.

next_steps: Review current scholarship on the Harley 7333, Additional 9066, Kk.1.6, and de Worde English Gesta traditions rather than projecting witness dates onto translation events.
Collate the complete English witnesses with modern work on the Latin Gesta circulation in England and distinguish common textual affiliation from direct physical-exemplar claims.
Retain anonymous plural responsibility and broad English cultural origin until agent-, date-, dialect-, and source-specific evidence supports narrower statements.
openQuestions.question: Who translated or adapted the distinct Middle English streams, when and where were they produced, and which Latin exemplars or textual branches underlie each one?

next_steps: Map the XML NOTE1 sequence to Herrtage's printed footnotes and typographic sigla.
Compare the corresponding passages with Madden's 1838 reading and complete witness images when obtainable.
Do not assign generic variant notes wholesale to Kk.1.6, Additional 9066, or Madden without note-level evidence.
openQuestions.question: Which source or editorial layer underlies each of the 1,347 XML NOTE1 elements marked TYPE=variant?

next_steps: Compare Herrtage Text III, Madden's 1838 text, and complete images of St John's A.2.18(4) line by line.
Review the 1507-1508? redating evidence cited by ESTC and preserve USTC's [1510] as a distinct older convention rather than averaging the two.
Confirm whether USTC's unshelfmarked Cambridge University Library holding is a distinct original copy, reproduction, or legacy record before revising survival claims.
openQuestions.question: Exactly how did Herrtage and Lucy Toulmin Smith alter Madden's eight Text III readings after comparison with the St John's de Worde print, and what date and surviving-copy statement should current cataloging prefer?

notes: The statement is intentionally broader than a place-of-composition or dialect localization claim.

scope.description: Middle English textual traditions and the English early print brought together in the compilation

notes: Associated place is used because a London imprint is not evidence that every stage of compilation occurred in London.

scope.description: Publication place of the cataloged 1879 compilation

responsibilities.agentName: Anonymous Middle English translators and adapters
responsibilities.display_name: Anonymous Middle English translators and adapters
notes: The reviewed sources do not identify one translator for all versions. Translator and adapter are collective descriptive roles, not an assertion of common authorship or a single translation event.
The relation between the English versions and particular Latin exemplars is not reduced here to one direct source manuscript.

scope.description: The distinct Middle English manuscript and early-print versions assembled in Herrtage's compilation
Fresh manuscript collation, revised reading text, parallel-version arrangement, notes, glossary, index, and other editorial work in the 1879 volume

notes: Adapter is a collective functional description of the transmitted English versions, not an assertion that every passage departs from a recoverable Latin source in the same way or that one agent shaped all three sections.

scope.description: Selection, reshaping, vernacular retelling, and moralization within the fluid English Gesta traditions represented by the compilation

notes: Compiler here describes the modern edited aggregate and its arrangement; it does not make Herrtage compiler of the medieval Gesta tradition.

scope.description: The 1879 edition-defined combination and ordering of Harley, Additional, Kk.1.6, and de Worde streams into Texts I-III

notes: Herrtage's acknowledgment does not say that she independently edited every passage or all Kk.1.6 material.

scope.description: Most Harley/Additional proof sheets and comparison of the eight de Worde selections with the St John's original

notes: Madden is the editor of the predecessor layer, not a named co-editor of the 1879 publication. Herrtage explicitly distinguishes his fresh recollation and revision from a simple reprint.

scope.description: Earlier editorial readings, arrangement, and notes retained or revised within Herrtage's 1879 successor compilation

subjects.label: Virtues and vices
notes: The moralizations interpret behavior through recurring discussions of sin, penance, charity, obedience, justice, pride, avarice, lust, and other virtues and vices.

subjects.label: Classical, biblical, and medieval narrative traditions
notes: The collection reuses Roman and classical names, biblical and saints' material, folklore, romance motifs, wonders, and social exempla; this is a broad repertory description, not a claim that every story is historically Roman.

subjects.label: Justice, providence, and moral conduct
notes: Recurring tales concern rulers, judges, families, vows, rewards, punishments, conversion, confession, death, salvation, and providential reversal.

summaries.value: Herrtage's 1879 compilation presents several late-medieval English versions of the Gesta Romanorum in a deliberately comparative arrangement. Its short prose narratives draw on classical and biblical names, legends, miracles, wonders, romance and folktale motifs, domestic and political dilemmas, and exemplary reversals. Most stories proceed to a Christian moralization that interprets emperors, knights, animals, objects, journeys, crimes, and rewards as figures for God, Christ, the Church, confession, penance, temptation, virtue, vice, judgment, death, and salvation. The edition's importance lies as much in its juxtaposition of divergent Harley, Additional, and Kk.1.6 streams and de Worde selections as in any single normalized tale sequence.

Text I contains 70 Harley MS 7333 story groups on printed pp. 1-326, with selected Additional MS 9066 versions printed in parallel. Text II contains 66 Additional-based groups on pp. 327-428, with eleven full Kk.1.6 parallel versions in its opening portion and other comparative readings. Text III contains eight stories on pp. 429-444 associated with Wynkyn de Worde's early print. Parallel streams make 180 XML DIV2 elements but do not increase the edition-level story-group inventory beyond 144.

The U-M/General Corpus representation retains a constructed title page and Herrtage's edited texts on printed pp. 1-444. It omits the contents, introduction, comparative table, later notes and illustrations, appendix, glossary, and index, even though those omitted portions remain essential evidence for editorial method and source identification. The cached 613-page PDF is the complete volume and is not cropped to the encoded work portion.

The record is intentionally edition-level. The three sections combine versions from distinct witnesses and an early print, and Herrtage reshapes Madden's predecessor edition through fresh collation, revised readings, parallel presentation, and added reference apparatus. Treating GRom as one anonymous medieval composition would collapse textual work, physical witness, editorial compilation, digital encoding, repository artifact, and facsimile into a false single layer.

notes: The preferred discovery title follows the 1879 edition and U-M 245-style title while omitting only the initial article found on the printed title page.
titles.value: Early English Versions of the Gesta Romanorum

notes: Title-page form in the 1879 volume.
titles.value: The Early English Versions of the Gesta Romanorum

notes: This is the broad U-M header uniform title. It is retained for discovery but is too general to define the cataloging boundary by itself.
Modern compact discovery form; not a historical title statement.

publication.embeddedTitle: THE EARLY ENGLISH VERSIONS OF THE Gesta Romanorum. — FORMERLY EDITED BY SIR FREDERIC MADDEN FOR THE ROXBURGHE CLUB, AND NOW RE-EDITED FROM THE MSS. IN BRITISH MUSEUM (HARL. 7333 & ADDIT. 9066) AND UNIVERSITY LIBRARY, CAMBRIDGE (Kk. 1. 6), — With Introduction, Notes, Glossary, &c.,

subjects.label: Classical, biblical, and medieval narrative traditions

work.summary: Herrtage's 1879 compilation presents several late-medieval English versions of the Gesta Romanorum in a deliberately comparative arrangement. Its short prose narratives draw on classical and biblical names, legends, miracles, wonders, romance and folktale motifs, domestic and political dilemmas, and exemplary reversals. Most stories proceed to a Christian moralization that interprets emperors, knights, animals, objects, journeys, crimes, and rewards as figures for God, Christ, the Church, confession, penance, temptation, virtue, vice, judgment, death, and salvation. The edition's importance lies as much in its juxtaposition of divergent Harley, Additional, and Kk.1.6 streams and de Worde selections as in any single normalized tale sequence.
